coredata.dc:title: Porous g-C3N4 tubes in-situ anchored by waste biomass-derived carbon dots for photocatalytic reduction of sunset yellow and Escherichia coli in juice
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shisen
Liu, Ping
Wang, Yinghui
Li, Yue
Ren, Yanting
Yang, Qingli
Ma, Yongchao
coredata.dc:description: Chemical hazardous substances and hazardous pathogenic bacteria pose a serious risk to human health. The development of efficient, safe and simple removal technologies is urgent. In this study, waste biomass derived carbon dots in-situ anchored porous g-C3N4 tubes were prepared by supramolecular self-assembly method. The prepared photocatalyst exhibited enhanced light absorption, large surface area, and abundant reactive sites. Photoelectrochemical analyses confirmed that the introduction of CDs could effectively promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers with enhanced degradation of sunset yellow (95.4 %) and inactivation of Escherichia coli (98.3 %) under visible light. Furthermore, the development of g-C3N4 loaded floatable porous alginate sponges for the simultaneous removal of E. coli and sunset yellow from fruit juices, obtaining efficient purification results and satisfactory overall acceptability. This work provides a feasible pathway for photocatalytic decontamination of contaminated liquid food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
g-C3N4 tubes
Sunset yellow
Bacteria
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of SY photodegradation and antimicrobial by CSTCN-3
Photocatalytic SY degradation and antibacterial activity in fruit juice
Characterizations and properties of as-prepared photocatalysts
Possible mechanism of photodegradation and sterilization by the CSTCN-3
Application of as-prepared CSTCN-3 in juice

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2021QE064), the Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (665-1120018, 663-1120082) and the Qingdao People' s Livelihood Science and Technology Plan Project (24-1-8-xdny-16-nsh).

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong ( ZR2021QE064 ) and Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University ( 665-1120018 , 663-1120082 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CSTCN-3. (b) SEM image and (c–d) TEM image of STCN. (e) SEM image and (f–g) TEM image of CSTCN-3. (i) HRTEM (Inset is a SAED image), and (j–m) corresponding TEM-EDX elemental mappings of CSTCN-3.
(a) XRD patterns (b) FT-IR spectra, (c) UV–vis absorption spectra, (d) PL spectra, (e) EIS plots, (f) transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples as indicated.
High-resolution XPS spectra of STCN and CSTCN-3. (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s (c) O 1s. (d) Electronic structure with optimized HOMO and LUMO energy of CSTCN.

(a) Photodegradation performance of STCN and CSTCN-3. (b) Time-dependent absorbance of SY in the presence of CSTCN-3. (c) Photocatalytic degradation effects of SY using the CSTCN-3 photocatalyst with the addition of scavengers under visible light. (d) Antimicrobial effectivity of STCN and CSTCN-3 against E. coli. (e) Photographs of E. coli on CSTCN-3 for 180 min visible light irradiation time. (f) Photocatalytic lethality efficiency on E. coli by CSTCN-3 in conjunction with various scavengers. 1 mM TEMPO, 1 mM IPA and 1 mM EDTA. (g) The SEM image of the E. coli. (h-j) Corresponding SEM images of E. coli treated by CSTCN-3.

(a) Kubelka-Munk function transformed differential spectra. (b) Mott-Schottky plot. (c) Valance band XPS spectra of STCN and CSTCN-3. (d) Proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic reduction of SY and E. coli by CSTCN-3.

(a) Synthetic procedures of SA@CSTCN sponge. (b) Digital photographs of SA sponge and SA@CSTCN sponge. (c-d) SEM images of SA@CSTCN sponge surface and cross section. (e) FT-IR spectra of SA sponge, SA@CSTCN sponge and CSTCN-3. The simultaneous removal of (f) SY and (g) E. coli from fruit juice by SA@CSTCN sponge.

head.ce:title: Porous g-C3N4 tubes in-situ anchored by waste biomass-derived carbon dots for photocatalytic reduction of sunset yellow and Escherichia coli in juice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical hazardous substances and hazardous pathogenic bacteria pose a serious risk to human health. The development of efficient, safe and simple removal technologies is urgent. In this study, waste biomass derived carbon dots in-situ anchored porous g-C3N4 tubes were prepared by supramolecular self-assembly method. The prepared photocatalyst exhibited enhanced light absorption, large surface area, and abundant reactive sites. Photoelectrochemical analyses confirmed that the introduction of CDs could effectively promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers with enhanced degradation of sunset yellow (95.4 %) and inactivation of Escherichia coli (98.3 %) under visible light. Furthermore, the development of g-C3N4 loaded floatable porous alginate sponges for the simultaneous removal of E. coli and sunset yellow from fruit juices, obtaining efficient purification results and satisfactory overall acceptability. This work provides a feasible pathway for photocatalytic decontamination of contaminated liquid food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The chemical hazardous substances such as synthetic dyes are widely used in food and beverage industries due to the advantages of bright color, strong coloring ability, stable nature, cheap and easy to obtain [1–3]. As one of the synthetic dyes, sunset yellow (SY) is extensively applied in various foods such as fruit juice, tea, candy, canned and ice cream to stimulate the appetite. However, excessive intake of SY is known to cause many health problems such as diarrhea, asthma, liver and kidney damage. Long-term consumption in large quantities can lead to serious diseases such as cancer [4]. Many countries and regions have limited its usage to about 0.1 g/kg. In addition, the problem of microbial contamination has resulted in significant economic losses to the food industry [5]. The microbial spoilage of food accounts for the largest proportion of food losses worldwide [6]. Microorganisms and their metabolites are often highly toxic and pathogenic, seriously threatening human health [7]. Therefore, developing efficient, safe and simple strategies for effective control chemical hazardous factors and microorganisms in food is necessary for food safety and economic efficiency.

Solar energy is the cleanest and most abundant energy source in nature. Semiconductor photocatalytic degradation of food hazardous factors driven by solar light is one of the most promising technologies for protecting food safety. To date, TiO2 [8], Bi2WO6 [9], g-C3N4 [10], Ti3C2 [11], etc. have been extensively used as photocatalysts. Among them, graphite-phase carbon nitride has received a great deal of attention from scholars due to its favorable environmental compatibility, tunable energy bands and visible light response range. However, insufficient light absorption and severe photogenerated carrier recombination efficiencies limit the improvement of photocatalytic activity. Various modification strategies have been developed to overcome these problems such as morphology modulation, elemental doping and constructing heterogeneous interfaces.

In particular, g-C3N4 nanotubes with hollow structure provide large specific surface area, rich active sites and efficient mass transfer process. Thus, it possesses more excellent photoelectrochemical properties than that of bulk materials [12]. In addition, as an affordable and biocompatible novel carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit excellent optical properties such as extended absorption, high electrical conductivity and electron donors and acceptors [13,14]. Therefore, the combination of carbon dots and g-C3N4 nanotubes could be a superb way to improve the catalytic performance of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

On the other hand, traditional g-C3N4 photocatalysts are mostly powder materials [15], which cause some problems such as poor dispersion, hard to recycle and susceptibility to secondary contamination [16], seriously limiting their application in food industry. To facilitate large-scale applications, several strategies have been developed, such as loading photocatalysts onto cellulose [17], textiles [18], and other substrates, or incorporating magnetic properties [19]. Sodium alginate (SA) is a natural polysaccharide with good biocompatibility [20,21]. It can be cross-linked and solidified to obtain three-dimensional porous sponge with excellent mechanical strength and high porosity [22]. Currently, sodium alginate sponge has been widely used in the fields of medicine [23,24], detection [25] and adsorption [26,27]. However, the application in the food industry is barely reported.

In this work, we design and construct a safe and recoverable visible photocatalytic system to achieve efficient removal of hazardous factors from fruit juices with the help of suitable carriers. The CDs were obtained from expired orange juice by a hydrothermal method, and subsequently CDs-modified carbon nitride nanotube (CSTCN) was elaborately prepared by a supramolecular self-assembly method. The introduction of CDs facilitated the expansion of light absorption and promoted charge separation and transfer. For verification, various characterization methods were used to confirm the successful fabrication of CSTCN, and to investigate the photocatalytic activity, photoelectric properties, and reaction mechanisms of CSTCN. Finally, the developed CSTCN nanotubes were incorporated into the sodium alginate (SA) gel matrix as filler to obtain a SA@CSTCN sponge. Further, the efficient removal of sunset yellow and Escherichia coli from fruit juices was verified, which provides a new strategy for the application of photocatalytic technology in liquid food.

Melamine, trithiocyanuric acid, sodium alginate, calcium chloride (CaCl2), p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetate acid (EDTA) and potassium bromide (KBr) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sunset yellow, isopropanol (IPA), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) were procured from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). LB broth and LB agar were purchased from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Qingdao, China). All chemicals were used as received without further treatment. The orange juice nearing their expiration date was purchased from a supermarket and stored until it expired.

The morphology and microstructure of photocatalysts and E. coli were observed by S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) instrument (Hitachi Ltd., Japan) and HT7700 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The crystal structures of the prepared samples were determined by D8QUEST X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Corp., Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected by a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). The light absorption properties were recorded by UV-3900 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan) and band gap values were calculated by Tauc plot method. Steady-state photoluminescence spectra (PL) were obtained at 370 nm excitation by F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan). Time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) was measured by the FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). Electrochemical measurements were performed by the CHI660e electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). ITO electrode serves as the working electrode, 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte. The Ag/AgCl electrode was selected as the reference electrode, and the Pt sheet electrode was used as the counter electrode. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples was analyzed using an Escalable250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA).

CDs derived from expired orange juice were synthesized via a typical hydrothermal method. Specifically, 30 ml of expired orange juice was extracted into a 100 ml Teflon lined reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling, the product obtained from the reaction was centrifuged and filtered through a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone filter to form a transparent brownish yellow solution of CDs.

In a typical procedure, 0.01 mol melamine and 0.01 mol trithiocyanuric acid were added to 60 ml deionized water and stirred for 6 h. The terminal amino groups of melamine and the terminal sulfhydryl groups of trithiocyanuric acid were self-assembled via hydrogen bonding interactions to obtain a homogeneous mixture [28]. Then a certain volume (1, 3, 5 and 7 mL) of the CDs solution was introduced to the above mixed solution and continued stirring overnight. The oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CDs were self-assembled with the unreacted amino groups via hydrogen bonding to facilitate the followed amidation reaction [29,30]. The mixture was filtered, washed, dried and transferred to a muffle furnace and calcined at 600℃ for 4 h with a ramping rate of 3 °C/min to obtain different ratios of CSTCN-x photocatalysts (x = 1, 3, 5 and 7 ml CDs solution). As comparison, STCN was prepared by the same conditions without the addition of CDs solution.

0.1 g photocatalyst was added to 50 ml deionized water and ultrasonicated for 30 min to make it uniformly dispersed. Subsequently, 0.75 g of sodium alginate was added and stirred vigorously for 6 h. The well-mixed pre-gel solution was transferred to a mold. Then freeze overnight and freeze-dry for 24 h. The obtained solid sponge was immersed in 300 ml of 3 wt% CaCl2 solution for 6 h to gel. After thorough washing with deionized water and freeze-dry for 36 h resulted in CSTCN-3-attached sodium alginate gel tablets, which were named SA@CSTCN. In addition, SA sponge was prepared by the same procedure without the addition of photocatalyst.

In a typical procedure, 30 mg of the CSTCN photocatalyst was mixed with 100 ml of SY solution (10 mg/L). The adsorption–desorption equilibrium was reached by stirring for 30 min under the dark. Then the reaction system was transferred to a 300w Xe lamp (Prefect Light, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter for photodegradation reaction (λ > 420 nm), the temperature of cooling water was kept at 5 ℃. Every 30 min, 3 ml of reaction solution was removed, centrifuged and its absorbance at 482 nm was determined. After one reaction, the photocatalyst was collected by centrifugation. Subsequently, the precipitate was washed and dried for the next experiment. The catalytic stability of the photocatalysts was evaluated by cycling four times.

Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922, Gram-negative) was selected as a typical research object. E. coli was thawed and transferred into LB broth medium which had been sterilized. The culture was incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C at 110 rpm for overnight. Then, the bacterial solution was washed with sterile saline by centrifugation several times to remove bacterial metabolites. Finally, the concentration of the bacterial suspension was adjusted to 1.0 × 107 CFU/ml by plate colony counting method for further use. All glass instruments and culture media used in this experiment were sterilized at 121℃ for 30 min.

The antimicrobial effectiveness of the photocatalyst against E. coli was evaluated by plate colony counting method. For this purpose, 50 mg of photocatalyst was added to 30 ml of the above prepared bacterial suspension. The adsorption–desorption equilibrium was reached by stirring for 30 min under the dark. Then the reaction system was transferred to a 300w Xe lamp (Prefect Light, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter for photo reactivity. Every 45 min, 1 ml of reaction solution was taken. Each reaction solution was diluted to 104 times, and the diluted solutions were laid on LB agar plates. The antibacterial activity of the materials was reflected by the number of colonies on the plates after incubation at 37 °C for 20 h. The recycling antibacterial experiments were similar to the above photocatalytic degradation experiments. The procedure was repeated four times to assess the antibacterial stability of the photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of SY photodegradation and antimicrobial by CSTCN-3
ce:section.ce:para: For the photocatalytic degradation process, a free radical scavenging experiment was performed using p-benzoquinone (BQ) (1 mM), isopropanol (IPA) (1 mM), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (1 mM), which acted as the superoxide radical (·O2 –), hydroxide radical (·OH) and hole (h+) quenchers, respectively. At the end of the dark reaction, different quenchers were added to the reaction system. The free radical scavenging experiments of antibacterial activity was to replace BQ with 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) as the quencher of ·O2 – without changing other conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic SY degradation and antibacterial activity in fruit juice
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh orange juice was filtered and centrifuged to remove solid precipitates such as pulp and peel, then the supernatant was retained and diluted up for reserve. A certain amount of SY and E. coli suspension was added to 30 ml of treated orange juice to obtain a final concentration of 10 ppm and 1 × 107 CFU/ml, respectively. Then 1.5g of SA@CSTCN was added to the above mixed solution, and stirred under dark conditions for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Every 30 min, 1 ml of reaction solution was removed, centrifuged and its absorbance at 482 nm was determined. Meanwhile, another 1 ml of the sample was diluted and spread in LB agar plates. Finally, the SY degradation and antibacterial activity of SA@CSTCN in fruit juices were calculated separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and properties of as-prepared photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The micro-morphology of STCN and CSTCN-3 was visualized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 1 b shows that STCN has a nanorod structure. After doping of CDs, CSTCN-3 remained the nanorod structure, but a large number of holes are generated on the surface (Fig. 1e). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images can clearly show that both STCN and CSTCN-3 are hollow structures (Fig. 1c and f). While CSTCN-3 has a thinner wall (Fig. 1d and g). It may be attributed to the large amount of gas released during the calcination process. The magnified TEM image of CSTCN-3 (Fig. 1h) indicates that the CDs are successfully doped into the STCN. Lattice fringes belonging to the carbon (001) crystal plane appears in HRTEM (Fig. 1i), which further indicating the tight binding of CDs with STCN. However, the selected electron diffraction map (SAED) of STCN (Figure S1) has a sharper diffraction ring than that of CSTCN-3. It may be attributed to the introduction of CDs leading to a change of the crystallinity in STCN. In addition, the correlating TEM-EDX elemental mappings (Fig. 1j-m) of CSTCN-3 showed that C, N, O, and S were uniformly distributed in the sample. The results discussed above have confirmed the successful in-situ anchoring of CDs to g-C3N4 nanotubes.

Figure S2 shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of STCN and CSTCN-3. The prepared nanotubes both exhibited typical type IV isotherms. A clear H3 hysteresis loop appeared at P/P0 of 0.35–0.99, indicating the presence of micropores and mesopores in the structure. This result was further confirmed by the distribution of pores in the inset. The surface area of the CSTCN-3 (233.5 m2/g) is much higher than that of STCN (102.5 m2/g) due to the generation of pores. The larger surface area and pores facilitate the harvesting of solar light and provide more reactive active sites.

The CDs solution appears brownish yellow under daylight and emits green fluorescence when transferred to 365 nm UV light (Figure S3 inset). In addition, the UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs (Figure S3a) indicates that CDs provide obvious absorption of both UV and visible light. Notably, the emission wavelength of CDs experiences a significant redshift as the excitation wavelength increases (Figure S3b). This may be attributed to the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior brought by the quantum effect of CDs nanoparticles. The excitation-emission matrix image of CDs is shown in Figure S3c, supporting the optical properties with a single emission center and excitation-wavelength-dependence. In addition, the XRD images of CDs shows weak diffraction peaks (Figure S4a), which indicates its low crystallinity. In the FT-IR spectrum of Figure S4b, the broad peak in the range of 3400 cm−1-3500 cm−1 appearing is attributed to the presence of adsorbed water. The peaks located at 1631 cm−1, 1390 cm−1, and 1046 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=O, C-O, and the symmetry vibrations of C-O-C, respectively [31,32]. From this, it can be seen that a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups exist on the surface of CDs.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared g-C3N4 materials were investigated by XRD spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, all samples present similar two characteristic diffraction peaks. The broad peak at 13.1° corresponds to the in-plane repeated motif of the tri-s-triazine ring. The sharp peak at 27.3° assigned to the interplanar stacking of conjugated aromatic units. Similar to the XRD images, the FT-IR spectra of all the samples exhibit almost identical characteristic peaks. The sharp peak at 816 cm−1 is ascribed to the formation of heptazine. The multiple peaks at 1100–1750 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N heterocyclic ring. The wide peaks in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 are assigned to the adsorbed water and N–H stretching vibration. The CDs-modified CSTCN-x curves exhibit no significant changes compared with STCN, indicating that the crystal and molecular structure of STCN in the composites is not affected by CDs. Moreover, no characteristic peaks of CDs were observed in the CSTCN-x samples, which may be due to the lower content of CDs in the composites.

Enhanced photoabsorbance is one of the most effective ways to improve the catalytic properties of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 2c, the light absorption capacity of CSTCN-x composites is significantly enhanced due to the quantum effect of CDs. Furthermore, it is further enhanced with the increase of quantum dot concentration. The color of the samples gradually changed from yellow to brown. Photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) were used to evaluate the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers. As shown in Fig. 2d, after the introduction of CDs, CSTCN-x composites exhibit lower PL strength than that of STCN. It is shown that the introduction of CDs can effectively promote carrier separation. The TRPL decay curves were fitted by the three-exponential function (Figure S5). The fitting results reveal that CSTCN-3 (5.93 ns) possesses a shorter carrier lifetime than that of STCN (6.79 ns). Notably, compared with STCN, the short lifetimes (τ1 and τ2) contributions of CSTCN-3 are increased and the long lifetime (τ3) contribution is reduced, indicating that the nonradiative transfer of photogenerated carriers in CSTCN-3 is more efficient [33]. Electrochemical characterization was used to further evaluate the efficient photogenerated carrier separation and transfer efficiency in CSTCN-3. Fig. 2e shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the as-prepared catalysts. Compared with STCN, CSTCN-3 exhibits a shorter arc radius, indicating a smaller charge transfer resistance, and a more efficient charge separation efficiency. In addition, CSTCN-3 presents the highest photocurrent response intensity during repeated cycles of “on-off” illumination, indicating a faster carrier transport rate in the visible light. However, with the increased doping concentration of CDs, the photocurrent response intensity reduced, which may be attributed to the introduction of new recombination sites by the increase of CDs.

The XPS spectroscopy was used to analyze the surface components and elemental composition of as-prepared samples. The high resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 3 a can be deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies of 284.7 and 287.9 eV, attributed to C–C/C=C and N-C=N, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3b, the N1s spectrum can be fitted to three peaks located at 398.4, 399.9 and 401.1 eV. These peaks correspond to C-N=C, N-(C)3 and N-Hx, respectively. Fig. 2c presents three peaks obtained in the high-resolution O1s spectra attributed to C=O at 531.5 eV, C-O at 532.2 eV, and O–H/adsorbed water at 533.3 eV. It is noteworthy that compared with STCN, the contents of C=O and C-O on the surface of CSTCN-3 are significantly increased, which may be attributed to the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. Weak S-elemental signals are found in the high resolution S2p spectrum (Figure S6). The peak at 163.4 eV is ascribed to the S-N bond, while the peak at 162.8 eV is identified as the production of sulfur oxides [34]. In addition, all peaks of O1s in CSTCN-3 are shifted toward lower binding energy, indicating the presence of electron flow into CDs in the composites.

To visualize the electron distribution, first principle calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) according to the previous report [35]. We calculated the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the optimized CSTCN structure. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the pristine BCN are uniformly distributed [36], which leads to rapid recombination of photogenerated charge. As shown in Fig. 3d, the grafting of CDS onto the BCN structure significantly leads to a spatial separation of the relative HOMO and LUMO energy levels. Obviously, the HOMO of CSTCN is mainly concentrated in the grafting sites, while the LUMO is distributed on the CDs, so that the rapid recombination of carriers can be effectively reduced and improved the photocatalytic activity.

To demonstrate this improved photogenerated carrier separation efficiency, SY and E. coli were used as typical contaminants for monitoring the photocatalytic activity of as prepared g-C3N4, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the SY solution is stable in the absence of light or catalyst. Under the same conditions, the SY degradation rate of STCN was only 22.8 % after 120 min. In contrast, the relative concentration of SY in the CSTCN composites decreased sharply as a function of reaction time after CDs doping. Among them, the CSTCN-3 sample exhibited the highest SY degradation efficiency (95.4 %), which nearly achieved the complete degradation of SY. This confirms that the introduction of CDs significantly facilitated the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and effectively improved the photocatalytic activity. However, high concentrations of CDs lead to a decreased degradation rate, which may be attributed to the large number of reactive sites covered by CDs.

Fig. 4b shows the time-dependent absorption spectra of SY solution in the CSTCN-3 photocatalytic system. Obviously, the characteristic peak of SY at 482 nm decreases rapidly under visible light and it is nearly negligible after 120 min. Consequently, the color of SY solution gradually changes from yellow to colorless (inset of Fig. 4b). In addition, the active substances generated during the photocatalytic process were identified by free radical scavenging tests. P-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) are used as scavengers of ·O2 –, ·OH and h+, respectively. In Fig. 4c, the SY photodegradation shows barely affected with the presence of IPA and EDTA. In contrast, the degradation efficiency of CSTCN-3 is significantly inhibited by the addition of BQ, indicating that ·O2 – is the main active substance in the photodegradation process of SY.

Based on the above conclusions, CSTCN-3 and STCN were used to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of as-prepared photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 4d, both STCN and CSTCN-3 exhibits significant antibacterial activity against E. coli. After 180 min of visible light with STCN, the survival rate of E. coli is around 68.8 %, indicating that the generation of ROS resulted in moderate bacterial damage. The antimicrobial performance of CSTCN-3 composite is significantly enhanced due to the excellent photogenerated carrier separation efficiency. The survival rate of E. coli decreased rapidly, with a final survival rate of only 1.7 %, which is comparable to most reported photocatalysts (Fig. 4e) [37,38].

Fig. 4(g–j) shows SEM images of E. coli morphological changes before and after CSTCN-3 treatment. Before treatment, the morphology of E. coli was full and the surface was smooth. After photocatalytic treatment, the morphology of E. coli became shriveled and the surface appeared concave, indicating that the bacteria were damaged. According to previous report[5], 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) was used instead of BQ as a ·O2 – scavenger in the antibacterial experiments. Similar to the degradation experiments, ·O2 – plays a key role in bacterial inactivation, while holes and ·OH have little effect on the bactericidal performance.

To measure the photostability and repeatability of the photocatalysts for the degradation of SY and the inactivation of E. coli, four successive degradation and sterilization experiments were carried out under the same conditions, respectively. As illustrated in Figure S7a, no significant decrease in photocatalytic activity was observed after four repeated uses. XRD and FT-IR spectra were used to reveal the structural stability of the catalysts after cyclic degradation reactions. From Figure S7b and c, the relative peak positions of XRD and FT-IR were basically remained before and after photodegradation, indicating that the as prepared composites possessed high stability and good reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of photodegradation and sterilization by the CSTCN-3
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the photocatalytic SY degradation and sterilization mechanism of CSTCN-3 composites, the band gap values (Eg) were initially calculated using Tauc method from UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra. The Eg of STCN and CSTCN-3 were determined to be 2.98 eV and 2.79 eV, respectively (Fig. 5 a). The narrower band gap of CSTCN-3 indicates that more photogenerated carriers can be produced under visible light [39].

Fig. 5b shows the Mott-Schottky curves of STCN and CSTCN-3 at different frequencies. The positive slope of the curve demonstrates that both STCN and CSTCN-3 are n-type semiconductors [40]. In addition, the charge density of the n-type semiconductor photoanode can be obtained from the slope (a = 2/(qεε0Nd)) [36]. It is obvious that CSTCN-3 possesses a smaller slope, indicating that a higher carrier concentration is available.

To further investigate the effect of introduced CDs on changes of energy band positions, valence band potentials were obtained from VB to XPS spectra (Fig. 5c). The VB potentials of STCN and CSTCN-3 were determined as 2.12 eV and 1.77 eV (vs Ag/AgCl), respectively. According to the equation [41].

ENHE/V = Φ + X eV − 4.44(Φ(3.88 eV) are the electron work function of analyzer), the VB potentials of STCN and CSTCN-3 were recalculated as 1.56 V and 1.21 V (vs NHE), respectively. The conduction band position can be calculated using the equation
ECB = EVB − Eg. The CB potentials of STCN and CSTCN-3 are speculated to be −1.42 V and −1.58 V (vs NHE), respectively.

Based on the above-mentioned experimental results and analysis, the photocatalytic SY degradation and sterilization mechanism on CSTCN-3 was proposed. As shown in Fig. 5d, after the semiconductor is excited by visible light, CSTCN-3 can accept more photons due to its narrower bandgap, thus more electrons are susceptible excited into the conduction band. Moreover, the more negative conduction band potential indicates that the electrons in the CB of CSTCN-3 are more reductive. The CDs on the surface of CSTCN-3 can effectively attract and accumulate photogenerated electrons, improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers and effectively inhibiting recombination, which greatly enhanced the photocatalytic activity. The accumulated photogenerated electrons are mass-transferred to react with O2 adsorbed on the catalyst surface to generate ·O2 –. These ROS species with strong oxidative activity can lead to the breaking of chemical bonds in SY and the death of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of as-prepared CSTCN-3 in juice
ce:section.ce:para: For the CSTCN-3 with the best photocatalytic performance, we prepared SA@CSTCN sponges based on alginate gel, and it was applied to demonstrate the purification effect in fruit juice to exhibit the prospect of large-scale application. The preparation method was shown in Fig. 6 a, Through the freeze-drying process, the ice crystals inside the SA gel are sublimated, resulting in the formation of holes. Subsequently, Ca2+ are added to cross-link and solidify the gel, giving it a certain mechanical strength.

In the absence of photocatalysts, the prepared SA sponge shows translucency, indicating that light can pass through the SA gel matrix well. Compared with SA sponges, SA@CSTCN sponges show a yellow color, indicating a well-dispersed of catalyst (Fig. 6b). The SEM image of the SA@CSTCN sponge was shown in Fig. 6c and d. The larger holes not only facilitate the reflection of photons, but also provide more reactive active sites. In addition, the abundant holes endow the sponge with floating properties, which ensures the continuous supply of oxygen and promotes the generation of ROS. FT-IR spectra of the samples are shown in Fig. 6e. The peaks of the catalysts are retained after the formation of sponge, indicating that the chemical bonds of the materials are not disrupted by SA gelation.

Therefore, we used the developed SA@CSTCN sponge to remove sunset yellow and E. coli from the juice. As shown in Fig. 6f and g, with increasing illumination time, the SY concentration shows a rapid decrease, and the removal rate reaches 75.47 % in 120 min. At the same time, there is no colony growth in the plate at 90 min, indicating that E. coli has been completely removed. Thus, SA@CSTCN sponge proved to be successful in removing both sunset yellow and E. coli in orange juice.

Besides the simultaneous removal of SY and E. coli, we estimated the sensory properties of photocatalytic treated orange juice and the results are shown in Figure S8. The scoring results demonstrated no significant changes in appearance, viscosity, flavor and taste before and after optical treatment. This is similar to some recent reports [42].

In summary, we first synthesized waste biomass-derived CDs and introduced into STCN for visible light driven sunset yellow degradation and E. coli inactivation. Detailed characterization results indicate that the porous hollow tubular structure endows g-C3N4 with a large specific surface area and abundant reactive sites. Optical and electrochemical characterization revealed that the CDs can capture and store electrons from STCN CB, greatly improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, which realized the rapid degradation of sunset yellow and efficient inactivation of E. coli under visible light. Cycling degradation experiments have demonstrated excellent structural and cyclic stability. At last, CSTCN-3 was loaded into floatable porous alginate sponges for the simultaneous removal of E. coli and sunset yellow from fruit juices, obtaining highly efficient purification results and satisfactory overall acceptability. The designed waste biomass-derived carbon dots anchored g-C3N4 photocatalysts and alginate sponge carrier platforms show a great cost-effectiveness and promising large-scale applicability, while improving the photocatalytic activity and recycling convenience.

Shisen Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Ping Liu: Validation, Resources, Project administration. Yinghui Wang: Validation, Investigation. Yue Li: Writing – review & editing. Yanting Ren: Writing – review & editing. Qingli Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yongchao Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2021QE064), the Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (665-1120018, 663-1120082) and the Qingdao People' s Livelihood Science and Technology Plan Project (24-1-8-xdny-16-nsh).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.161584.
